Pixley Family Genealogy
FindAGrave

burial.plot_notes: Maplewood Cemetery Henrietta Monroe County NY per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived two husbands; died at Henrietta at eighty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Hooker Pixley buried her first husband Isaac Pixley in 1790 when she was thirty-six — left with six children, the youngest barely two — remarried James Root in 1793, bore another child, then buried Root in 1802 when she was forty-eight, and spent the next thirty-nine years a widow in Henrietta, New York, dying on January 6, 1841 at age eighty-seven. She had migrated from Woodbury, Connecticut to the Berkshire hills of Massachusetts as a young wife, and then west again to upstate New York as an old widow, the arc of her life tracking the westward movement of her entire generation — and she outlived every chapter of it, the Puritan inheritance carried through two husbands, seven children, and eighty-seven years.

burial.plot_notes: Maplewood Cemetery Henrietta Monroe County NY per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived two husbands; died at Henrietta at eighty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Hooker Pixley buried her first husband Isaac Pixley in 1790 when she was thirty-six — left with six children, the youngest barely two — remarried James Root in 1793, bore another child, then buried Root in 1802 when she was forty-eight, and spent the next thirty-nine years a widow in Henrietta, New York, dying on January 6, 1841 at age eighty-seven. She had migrated from Woodbury, Connecticut to the Berkshire hills of Massachusetts as a young wife, and then west again to upstate New York as an old widow, the arc of her life tracking the westward movement of her entire generation — and she outlived every chapter of it, the Puritan inheritance carried through two husbands, seven children, and eighty-seven years.

burial.plot_notes: Maplewood Cemetery Henrietta Monroe County NY per FA — NOTE: Isaac died at Great Barrington MA in 1790; burial at Henrietta NY CEM497 may be erroneous FA data entry or remains were reinterred by Abigail later

narrative_blocks.body: Isaac Pixley kept a tavern at Great Barrington, Massachusetts, that burned in 1787 — the Pixley genealogy notes the fire 'is said to have been incendiary,' suggesting arson — and after losing the building he built the old red house that David Humphrey would later occupy for many years; his wife's sister Anna Hooker, present at the fire, fractured her leg badly jumping from the second-story window to frozen ground. He died three years later, on May 11, 1790, at Great Barrington, age forty — leaving Abigail with six children under seventeen — and she would outlive him by fifty-one years, eventually dying in Henrietta, New York in 1841 at eighty-seven.

career.organization: Fabius, New York and elsewhere in New York State
career.notes: EH: 'He was a Baptist minister at Fabius, N.Y. and elsewhere in that state.' Died Jan 31, 1880 Preston Hollow NY.

narrative_blocks.header: His children all bore their Quaker mother's name
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Burling Pixley was born in Norwich, Connecticut in 1813 and became a Baptist minister serving congregations at Fabius and elsewhere in New York State — his denomination a different branch of Protestant dissent from the Puritan inheritance that ran through his Hooker ancestry. Three of his five children carried the surname 'Burling' as a middle name, preserving his Quaker wife Mary Burling's family long after she was buried in the Quaker Burying Ground in New York City.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

education.notes: Graduate of Berkshire Medical School per EH #2387. Berkshire Medical School in Pittsfield MA (1823-1867).

narrative_blocks.header: Berkshire Medical School graduate; died at twenty-five, unmarried
narrative_blocks.body: George Burling Pixley graduated from Berkshire Medical School — one of the New England proprietary medical schools of the antebellum era, operating in Pittsfield, Massachusetts from 1823 to 1867 — and died December 11, 1846 at Henrietta, New York, unmarried, at approximately twenty-five. His middle name Burling honored his mother Mary Burling Pixley's Quaker family, the same family from which Burling Slip in lower Manhattan took its name; he was the only one of John Hooker Pixley's children by Mary Burling to pursue a medical degree.

burial.plot_notes: East Bloomfield Cemetery Ontario County NY per FA. Epitaph: 'Age 80 Years. One of the early settlers of East Bloomfield, Ontario County, New York'

narrative_blocks.header: One of the early settlers of East Bloomfield
narrative_blocks.body: Ira Pixley was born at Great Barrington, Massachusetts in 1783 — in the same Berkshire hills where his parents' tavern burned in 1787 and his father died in 1790 — and by 1809 had moved his family west to East Bloomfield, Ontario County, New York, where he and Sarah Adams married that August and where he would spend the rest of his life. His gravestone at East Bloomfield Cemetery calls him simply 'one of the early settlers of East Bloomfield, Ontario County, New York' — the son of an innkeeper who died young, become himself a founding farmer of an upstate New York township, eighty years old when he died there in 1863.

narrative_blocks.body: Fanny Pixley married James Sperry in Great Barrington in January 1812 and they lived at Henrietta, New York, raising eight children across three decades; she died April 29, 1866 in Rochester at approximately seventy-eight, and James outlived her by almost exactly eleven months — dying March 29, 1867 also in Rochester. The Pixley genealogy corrects the EH date: Fanny died in 1866, not 1867 — she went first, after fifty-four years of marriage, and James followed in the same city the following spring.

career.notes: EH: 'she a teacher in his school' — taught at Henrietta Academy where her husband William Waring Latting was principal. Later moved to Rochester NY.

narrative_blocks.header: She taught in the school her husband ran
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Ann Sperry married William Waring Latting of Baltimore, Maryland, who was principal of Henrietta Academy in Monroe County, New York — and she became a teacher in his school, one of the few documented instances in this tree where a wife's professional role was specifically recorded in the genealogical source rather than erased by it. The couple eventually moved to Rochester, where she died in March 1896, having outlived a son James Sperry Latting and having named her daughter Minnie Elizabeth for the Sperry family that her Pixley mother carried into the marriage.

career.notes: Practicing physician at Rochester after Civil War discharge. Also served as health officer of the city of Rochester.

military_service.discharge_reason: Injuries received at the Battle of Swan's Quarter, North Carolina, April 30, 1863
military_service.role: Medical department officer; treated wounded through the 1862-1863 campaigns in coastal North Carolina
engagements: Battle of Swan's Quarter, NC (April 30, 1863) — coastal sound operation where Sperry was wounded

narrative_blocks.header: Discharged from the Civil War at Swan's Quarter
narrative_blocks.body: Dr. Moses Sperry served in the medical department of the Third New York Cavalry under Colonel Mix, treating wounded men through the campaigns of 1862-1863, until injuries he received at the Battle of Swan's Quarter, North Carolina forced his discharge in 1863 — a small, overlooked engagement in the coastal war, but the one that ended his service. He returned to Rochester, where he became a practicing physician and eventually health officer of the city, the same skill set he had deployed under fire in the Carolina sounds now directed at the public health of a peacetime industrial city on the Erie Canal.

narrative_blocks.header: Returned wounded; became Rochester city health officer
narrative_blocks.body: Discharged in 1863 from injuries at Swan's Quarter, Sperry returned to Rochester New York and resumed civilian medical practice. He served as Health Officer of the city — directing Rochester's public-health response to the cholera, smallpox, and tuberculosis threats of the Gilded Age industrial city on the Erie Canal. His wartime cavalry-medicine training carried directly into peacetime municipal sanitation work.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Henrietta, died Rochester, both Monroe County
narrative_blocks.body: Sperry's life arc traced a tight twelve-mile orbit within Monroe County New York. He was born April 1, 1830 in Henrietta and died September 24, 1902 in Rochester — the same county where he had practiced medicine for nearly forty postwar years. He married Olive Canfield of Bennington NY on January 8, 1857, five years before he marched south with the Third New York Cavalry.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Killed by a powder explosion at Battle Creek
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Sperry died December 10, 1892 at Battle Creek, Michigan — killed instantly by a powder explosion, according to EH, though the Pixley genealogy places the event in Joliet, Illinois in 1881, a discrepancy that has never been resolved. He had married Clarissa Delano Colvin in 1858, but she predeceased him by twenty-one years, dying in 1871 also at Battle Creek; by the time the explosion killed him, he had been a widower for two decades and left behind five children who ranged in age from twenty-three to thirty-three.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: His great-grandson won the Nobel Peace Prize
narrative_blocks.body: James Root's first wife was Lydia Anna Robb (1749-1788 of Westfield, Massachusetts), and their son Elihu Root Sr. (born 1772) began a family line that produced Oren Root (born 1803) and then Elihu Root (born 1845) — Secretary of War under President McKinley, Secretary of State under Theodore Roosevelt, and winner of the Nobel Peace Prize in 1912 for his work in international arbitration. When James Root then married Abigail Hooker as his second wife in 1793, he connected his family to the descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker — meaning the Pixley-Hooker family and the Elihu Root line share a common progenitor in James Root, though the Nobel laureate descended from Lydia Robb's branch and carries no Hooker blood.

burial.plot_notes: Lyons Township Cemetery Lyons Ionia County Michigan per FA

narrative_blocks.body: John Amos Root moved with his family to Lyons township, Ionia County, Michigan in 1840 when he was about eight or nine years old — the family having come west from Moscow, New York — and spent the next sixty-one years there, farming the same land where he died, serving as director of the Farmers' Mutual Fire Insurance Company of Ionia County and officer of his school district. He married Sarah March on June 4, 1856, and his obituary in the Lyons paper noted that 'by his kindly nature and pleasant ways he had endeared himself to all with whom he came in contact,' closing with the motto he had followed: 'Do Unto Others as ye would that they should do unto you.'

migration.notes: Among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish, Woodbury. One of the leading men of the town for many years.

narrative_blocks.header: Among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1720 he was among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish in Woodbury, Connecticut. He was described as 'for many years one of the leading men of the town.'

Edward Hooker's record says he 'died suddenly' in February 1756, at age sixty-seven. The unexpected death cut short his role as one of the leading men of Bethlehem Parish — a settlement he had helped establish thirty-five years before.

bio.notes: Also spelled Curtiss in some records
